ingredients_notes: Rice noodles stir-fried in a wok with tamarind paste, fish sauce, and palm sugar sauce. Typically includes eggs, firm tofu and/or shrimp or chicken, bean sprouts, green onions, and dried shrimp. Finished with crushed peanuts, lime wedge, and chilli flakes. Cooked over high heat for characteristic smoky wok hei flavour.

ingredients_notes: Classic clear broth (nam sai) version prepared with fresh prawns, lemongrass, galangal, kaffir lime leaves, Thai bird's eye chillies, fish sauce, lime juice, and mushrooms. The creamy version (nam khon) uses evaporated milk or coconut milk and adds approximately 60–90 kcal and 4–6g of additional fat per bowl.

ingredients_notes: Traditionally prepared with beef or chicken, potatoes, onions, roasted peanuts, and coconut milk, simmered in a fragrant curry paste made from dried chilies, lemongrass, galangal, cinnamon, cardamom, cloves, and star anise. Seasoned with fish sauce (nam pla). Served with steamed jasmine rice.

ingredients_notes: Steamed jasmine rice (~180g) as primary carbohydrate source; minced pork (~120g) providing protein, fat, B vitamins, and zinc; one large fried egg (~50g) contributing protein and cholesterol; holy basil leaves (~10g) for antioxidants and Vitamin K; garlic and fresh chilies (~10g) for Vitamin C and capsaicin; oyster sauce (~15ml) and fish sauce (~10ml) as primary sodium contributors; vegetable cooking oil (~15ml) for unsaturated fats; and a small amount of palm or white sugar (~5g).

ingredients_notes: Primary ingredients include shredded unripe green papaya, dried shrimp, fish sauce (nam pla), palm sugar, lime juice, fresh garlic, bird's eye chillies, cherry tomatoes, yard-long beans, and roasted peanuts. ingredients_notes: Approximately 150g steamed glutinous (sticky) rice soaked in sweetened salted coconut milk, 120g fresh ripe mango (typically Nam Dok Mai or Ok Rong variety), and 80ml coconut milk sauce with added sugar and salt. Optionally garnished with toasted sesame seeds or mung beans.

Seasoned with dark soy sauce, light soy sauce, oyster sauce, and a touch of sugar. Cooked in vegetable oil or lard using high-heat wok frying technique to achieve wok hei flavour.

Nutritional values may vary if protein is substituted with beef (higher fat), prawns (higher cholesterol), or tofu (lower fat and protein).

Rice is cooked in seasoned chicken broth, contributing additional sodium and collagen.

ingredients_notes: Key ingredients include pork belly or pork shoulder (bone-in), Hang Lay curry paste (dried chilies, shrimp paste, lemongrass, galangal, turmeric), tamarind paste, palm sugar, pickled garlic, fresh ginger, shallots, fish sauce, and optionally peanuts. Some modern versions may include a small amount of coconut milk. No coconut milk is used in the most traditional preparation.

ingredients_notes: Primary ingredients include white fish paste (commonly Spanish mackerel or snapper), red curry paste (Nam Prik Gaeng Daeng), finely sliced kaffir lime leaves, thinly sliced long beans, fish sauce (Nam Pla), egg as a binder, palm sugar, and vegetable oil for deep frying. Served with a sweet chilli cucumber dipping sauce (Ajad) containing cucumber, shallots, chilli, vinegar, and sugar. Dipping sauce contributes approximately 35-40 kcal and most of the listed sugar content.

Fresh mint and coriander (~15g) are used as garnish and flavour enhancers.

Moderate sugar content from palm sugar; notable for those monitoring glycemic intake
Excellent source of lean protein supporting muscle repair and satiety
Rich in Vitamin B12 and Selenium supporting neurological health and antioxidant defense
Good source of Omega-3 fatty acids if prepared with oily fish such as snapper or sea bass
Individuals managing hypertension or kidney conditions should consume with caution due to high sodium
data_source_note: Estimated based on authentic Thai preparation methods using whole fried fish approximately 200g cooked weight with approximately 3-4 tbsp of chili sauce. Nutritional values may vary depending on fish species, oil absorption during frying, and sauce quantity used. Percent Daily Values based on a 2,000 kcal diet.

vitamin_a_iu.notes: Beta-carotene from mango; supports eye health

ingredients_notes: Key ingredients include glutinous (sticky) rice (~150g cooked) prepared with coconut milk and sugar, fresh ripe mango slices (~150g), and a lightly salted sweet coconut cream topping (~60-80ml). Optional garnishes include toasted mung beans and/or toasted sesame seeds.
dietary_considerations: Vegan and vegetarian friendly - entirely plant-based with no animal products
Gluten-free - glutinous rice contains no gluten despite its name; safe for coeliac disease or gluten sensitivity
Dairy-free - uses coconut milk and coconut cream, not dairy
High in saturated fat (~13-16g) from coconut milk and coconut cream; may be a concern for those monitoring cardiovascular health
High in simple sugars (~38-42g) from added sugar in sticky rice preparation and natural sugars in ripe mango
High glycaemic load due to glutinous rice and ripe mango; individuals managing blood sugar levels such as Type 2 diabetes should consume mindfully
Moderate sodium (~180-220mg) from salted coconut cream topping; worth noting for those on low-sodium diets
Coconut allergen warning - coconut is classified as a tree nut by some regulatory bodies including the FDA; individuals with tree nut allergies should exercise caution
Potential sesame allergen - if garnished with toasted sesame seeds; confirm with vendor
Mung beans occasionally used as garnish; generally low allergen risk
data_source_note: Estimated based on authentic Thai preparation methods and standard ingredient quantities for a typical 1-plate serving (~360-380g total). Actual values may vary depending on the restaurant, portion size, and specific recipe used.

calcium_mg.sources: shrimp with shells, fish paste, vegetables

ingredients_notes: Tamarind or green mango-based broth with pounded chilli paste (prik gaeng som), shrimp or white fish as primary protein, fish sauce (nam pla), shrimp paste (kapi), and vegetables such as green papaya, cabbage, or morning glory. Contains no coconut milk.
dietary_considerations: Contains shellfish — shrimp and shrimp paste present; high allergen risk
Contains fish — fish sauce used in almost all versions; not suitable for fish allergies
Generally gluten-free — verify fish sauce brand for additives
High sodium content — 900–1,200 mg per bowl (~39–52% of 2,300 mg daily recommended limit); caution advised for those managing hypertension or kidney conditions
Not suitable for vegetarians or vegans — contains shrimp paste and fish sauce
Suitable for pescatarians
Dairy-free — no dairy ingredients used
Low-fat — one of the leanest Thai curry options due to absence of coconut milk
Low-calorie — suitable for calorie-conscious diets
Keto and low-carb suitability is moderate and depends on vegetables used
data_source_note: Estimated based on authentic Thai preparation methods and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central, Thai food composition databases, and established culinary nutrition references. Actual values may vary based on specific ingredients, cooking methods, and portion sizes.

iron_mg.notes: From dried shrimp, tofu and noodles

vitamin_c_mg.notes: From bean sprouts and lime/citrus garnish

ingredients_notes: Deep-fried rice vermicelli noodles tossed in a sweet-sour-savoury sauce made from tamarind, fish sauce, palm sugar, and citrus. Garnished with bean sprouts, egg ribbons, dried shrimp or prawns, tofu, and fresh coriander.
dietary_considerations: Contains shellfish (dried shrimp/prawns)
Contains eggs
Contains soy (tofu)
Contains fish (fish sauce)
May contain gluten if soy sauce is used in the sauce base
High sugar content due to palm sugar and tamarind — not suitable for low-sugar or blood glucose management diets
High sodium from fish sauce and dried shrimp — caution advised for low-sodium or hypertensive diets
Not suitable for vegan or vegetarian diets in traditional form
Potentially gluten-free if fish sauce is used exclusively instead of soy sauce — confirm with restaurant
Not recommended for low-sugar diets
Consume in moderation on low-sodium diets
data_source_note: Estimated based on authentic Thai preparation methods. Values referenced from USDA FoodData Central, Thai culinary nutritional studies, and established food composition databases. Nutritional values may vary depending on the restaurant, portion size, and specific recipe variations.

ingredients_notes: Coarsely minced pork stuffed in natural pork casings, seasoned with lemongrass, galangal, kaffir lime leaves, dried chilies, shallots, garlic, shrimp paste (kapi), and fish sauce. Grilled or pan-fried. Typically served with sticky rice, fresh vegetables, and nam prik noom (green chili dip).
dietary_considerations: High sodium: 900-1100mg per plate, approximately 40-48% of recommended daily intake
Contains shellfish: shrimp paste (kapi) is a core ingredient
Contains fish: fish sauce is commonly used
Contains pork: primary protein source
Contains alliums: shallots and garlic are key aromatics
Possible gluten: some recipes use soy sauce, preparation should be verified
Suitable for keto and low-carb diets due to very low carbohydrate and high fat content
Not suitable for halal diets due to pork content
Not suitable for vegetarian or vegan diets due to pork, fish sauce, and shrimp paste
Dairy-free
Not suitable for low-sodium diets
Paleo suitability is partial and depends on shrimp paste quality
Gluten-free status is conditional and requires verification of no soy sauce addition
If served with sticky rice (~150g) and fresh vegetables, total meal estimate rises to approximately 700-750 kcal
data_source_note: Estimated based on authentic Thai preparation methods and standard nutritional databases including USDA and Thai Food Composition Database. Actual values may vary by recipe, region, and cooking method.

dish_name: Thai Steamed Fish with Lime (Pla Neung Manao)
ingredients_notes: Whole steamed fish (sea bass, tilapia, or snapper; approx. 250–280g edible portion), fresh lime juice, fish sauce (nam pla), minced or sliced garlic, fresh bird's eye chillies (prik kee noo), lemongrass, galangal, coriander/cilantro garnish, a small amount of sugar, chicken or vegetable stock as steaming liquid, and optional glass noodles placed under the fish.
dietary_considerations: High sodium content — fish sauce contributes significantly; individuals with hypertension should monitor intake
Contains fish and fish sauce — not suitable for those with fish or seafood allergies
Generally gluten-free — verify fish sauce brand as some may contain wheat
Low in carbohydrates — suitable for low-carb diets when served without glass noodles
High lean protein source — ideal for muscle maintenance and repair
Low in saturated fat — fish provides beneficial omega-3 fatty acids supporting heart health
Not vegan or vegetarian — contains fish and fish sauce
Typically served with steamed jasmine rice which adds approximately 180 kcal per cup and is not included in the nutritional values above
data_source_note: Estimated based on authentic Thai preparation methods, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central, Thai food composition databases, and standard culinary portion references. Values may vary depending on fish species, fish size, and amount of sauce consumed.

ingredients_notes: Primary ingredients include pork ribs or offal as the main protein and fat source, lemongrass and galangal for antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties, kaffir lime leaves for aromatic oils, dried chilies for capsaicin and Vitamin A precursors, fish sauce as the primary sodium and umami contributor, toasted rice powder (khao khua) for slight carbohydrate and fiber addition, lime juice for Vitamin C, and fresh herbs such as sawtooth coriander and spring onions for micronutrient support including iron, Vitamin K, and folate.
dietary_considerations: Contains fish allergens from fish sauce — not suitable for fish or seafood allergies
High sodium content at 900–1100mg per bowl, representing approximately 40–48% of the recommended daily intake of 2300mg — caution advised for those managing hypertension or kidney conditions
Generally gluten-free in authentic preparation, but commercial fish sauces or seasoning pastes may contain trace gluten — verify labels before consumption
Traditional recipe is shellfish-free, however some regional Isaan variations may include dried shrimp (kung haeng)
Not suitable for vegetarians or vegans due to pork and fish sauce content
Not suitable for a low-sodium diet without significant modification
Suitable for gluten-free diets if fish sauce is verified gluten-free
Suitable for dairy-free diets
Suitable for paleo diets
Suitable for keto and low-carb diets at approximately 10–13g net carbohydrates per bowl
Halal-suitable only if pork is substituted with beef or chicken and halal fish sauce is used
If served with traditional sticky rice, add approximately 160–180 kcal and 35–38g of carbohydrates per serving
data_source_note: Estimated based on authentic Thai preparation using standard Isaan-style Tom Saep methods and established food composition databases including USDA and the Thai Food Composition Database. Actual values may vary based on restaurant preparation, ingredient ratios, and portion sizes.

ingredients_notes: Crispy shell made from rice flour and mung bean flour; filled with sweetened coconut cream, foi thong (golden egg yolk threads), meringue-like whipped coconut topping, and shredded coconut. Sweetened with palm sugar or white sugar. Batter traditionally flavored with coriander root and a small amount of salt.
dietary_considerations: Contains eggs — allergen risk
Contains coconut (tree nut) — allergen risk
Typically gluten-free due to rice flour base — verify cross-contamination at point of purchase
Not vegan — contains egg yolks and coconut cream
Suitable for vegetarians (sweet version only)
Moderate to high saturated fat from coconut cream
Moderate sugar content from palm sugar and egg yolk thread filling
Savory version contains shrimp — shellfish allergen risk
Low in protein — considered a treat rather than a nutritious snack
Moderate calorie density — easy to consume multiple pieces; monitor portion intake
Those managing blood sugar should be mindful of palm sugar and sweetened fillings
data_source_note: Estimated based on authentic Thai street food preparation of the sweet version (Khanom Buang). Values represent a midpoint average and may vary depending on vendor, shell thickness, and filling quantity.

dish_name: Thai Baked Prawns with Glass Noodles (Kung Ob Woon Sen / กุ้งอบวุ้นเส้น)

calcium_mg.note: Moderate — from prawn shells and heads

ingredients_notes: Key ingredients include whole shell-on prawns (200–220g) as the primary protein source, cooked glass noodles from approximately 60g dry mung bean vermicelli as the primary carbohydrate, oyster sauce (2–3 tbsp) and light soy sauce (1–2 tbsp) contributing sodium and umami, sesame oil (1–2 tsp) for healthy fats and flavour, fresh and fried garlic (15–20g), ginger (10g), lard or cooking oil (1–2 tsp), coriander root, white pepper, Chinese celery (15–20g), and a small amount of sugar (~1 tsp).
dietary_considerations: Contains shellfish (prawns) — major allergen; not suitable for shellfish allergy sufferers
Contains gluten — oyster sauce and soy sauce typically contain wheat; not gluten-free unless tamari or gluten-free alternatives are used
Contains sesame — sesame oil is present; relevant for sesame allergies
Contains soy — soy sauce is a key ingredient

High sodium — approximately 1,100–1,400 mg per serving, representing 48–61% of the recommended daily intake of 2,300 mg; caution advised for individuals managing hypertension, kidney disease, or cardiovascular conditions
Suitable for pescatarians
Dairy-free in standard preparation
Not suitable for low-carb or ketogenic diets due to high carbohydrate content from glass noodles
Not suitable for low-sodium diets in standard preparation
Nut-free in standard preparation
Halal status requires verification of oyster sauce brand compliance
Moderate-to-high cholesterol content due to prawns; dietary cholesterol has less impact on blood cholesterol than saturated fat for most individuals
Relatively low in saturated fat compared to many other Thai dishes
Glass noodles are low in fibre and micronutrients but provide quick-release energy
data_source_note: Estimated based on authentic Thai preparation methods and standard ingredient quantities. Actual values may vary depending on restaurant preparation, prawn size, noodle quantity, and sauce amounts used.

dish_name: Shrimp Paste Fried Rice (Khao Khluk Kapi)

sugars_g.note: Primarily from sweet pork (moo wan) and green mango

ingredients_notes: Jasmine rice stir-fried with fermented shrimp paste (kapi) in coconut oil or lard. Served with traditional accompaniments including sweet caramelised pork (moo wan), shredded omelette, green mango, shallots, dried shrimp, long beans, and fresh bird's eye chillies. Day-old rice is traditionally used to increase resistant starch content and improve texture.
dietary_considerations: Very high sodium content (1,400–1,800 mg per plate; 60–78% of recommended daily intake) — not suitable for low-sodium or hypertension diets without significant modification
Contains shellfish (major allergen) — shrimp paste and dried shrimp present throughout dish
Contains eggs — shredded omelette included as standard accompaniment
Contains pork — sweet pork (moo wan) is a core component; not suitable for pescatarians or those avoiding pork
Possible gluten presence — some sweet pork recipes use soy sauce; verify preparation if gluten-free diet is required
Possible fish allergen — some versions include fish sauce
Possible soy allergen — sweet pork marinade may contain soy-based ingredients
Not suitable for vegetarians or vegans — contains shrimp paste, dried shrimp, pork, and egg
Moderate to high cholesterol content (180–220 mg) — primarily from egg and dried shrimp
Elevated sugar content compared to standard fried rice — due to caramelised sweet pork and green mango
Dairy-free as traditionally prepared
Good source of Vitamin B12 (75–100% RDI) from fermented and dried seafood
Resistant starch benefit — use of day-old jasmine rice lowers glycaemic impact compared to freshly cooked rice
Capsaicin present from fresh bird's eye chillies — potential metabolic and anti-inflammatory benefits
data_source_note: Estimated based on authentic Thai preparation methods and established food composition databases including USDA, Thai Food Composition Database, and FAO. Actual nutritional values may vary depending on restaurant, portion size, and specific recipe variations.

iron_mg.source_note: From chicken, curry paste and turmeric

vitamin_a_ug_rae.source_note: From turmeric and yellow curry paste

ingredients_notes: Authentic Thai preparation using full-fat coconut milk (~165ml), yellow curry paste (~30g), chicken thigh as protein (~120g), potatoes (~80g), onions, bell peppers, fish sauce, palm sugar, and 1 cup cooked jasmine rice (~180g). Occasionally garnished with peanuts or cashews.
dietary_considerations: High in saturated fat — coconut milk is the primary contributor; individuals managing cardiovascular health should be mindful
High sodium — fish sauce and curry paste are significant sodium sources; may not be suitable for those on low-sodium diets
Calorie-dense — combination of coconut milk and rice makes this a relatively high-calorie meal
Contains fish and shellfish allergens — fish sauce and shrimp paste present in curry paste
May contain gluten — some commercial curry pastes may contain gluten; use certified gluten-free products if required
May contain tree nuts — occasionally garnished with peanuts or cashews
May contain soy — if tofu is used as the protein source
Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians in standard preparation — contains fish sauce and shrimp paste
Can be adapted for vegans using vegan curry paste and soy sauce or coconut aminos as substitute
Gluten-free if using certified gluten-free curry paste and fish sauce
Not suitable for low-fat or low-sodium diets without significant modification
data_source_note: Estimated based on authentic Thai preparation. Values sourced from USDA FoodData Central, Thai culinary nutritional references, and established food science data. Nutritional values may vary based on restaurant portioning, coconut milk fat content, and ingredient substitutions.

dish_name: Thai Water Chestnut Dessert (Tub Tim Krob)
ingredients_notes: Key ingredients include diced water chestnuts coated in red food colouring, tapioca starch for the chewy translucent coating, full-fat coconut milk lightly sweetened and salted, simple syrup made from sugar and water, pandan leaves for fragrance, crushed ice for serving, and optional jasmine water for floral aroma.
dietary_considerations: Vegan and vegetarian friendly — contains no animal products
Gluten-free — tapioca starch is naturally gluten-free; verify no cross-contamination
High in saturated fat — primarily from full-fat coconut milk; those monitoring cardiovascular health should be mindful
Moderate to high sugar content — due to added simple syrup and naturally sweet coconut milk; not ideal for diabetics or those on low-sugar diets
Contains artificial food colouring — traditional red colour from red food dye; individuals sensitive to artificial colourants should take note
Potential tree nut allergy concern — coconut is classified as a tree nut by some regulatory bodies such as the FDA
Relatively low sodium — generally well-tolerated for those on sodium-restricted diets
Served cold — may not be suitable for individuals with cold food sensitivities
data_source_note: Estimated based on authentic Thai preparation methods. Nutritional values may vary depending on the specific recipe, coconut milk fat content, and amount of syrup used.
